explanation.summary: Physiologic dead space = Anatomic dead space + Alveolar dead space. It represents the total volume of each breath that does not participate in gas exchange.
explanation.whyCorrect: Physiologic dead space encompasses all wasted ventilation: anatomic dead space (conducting airways) plus alveolar dead space (ventilated but unperfused alveoli). In healthy individuals, alveolar dead space is minimal, so physiologic dead space is approximately equal to anatomic dead space.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Tidal dead space is not a standard classification. The three types are anatomic, alveolar, and physiologic (their sum).
whyWrongByChoice.C: Residual volume is the air remaining in the lungs after maximal expiration. It is a lung volume, not a type of dead space.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Functional dead space is not a standard term. Physiologic dead space is the sum of anatomic and alveolar dead space.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Expiratory reserve volume and residual volume are lung volumes. They are not types of dead space.

questions.stem: The respiratory quotient (R) in the alveolar gas equation is normally 0.8. This value represents which of the following?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The ratio of tidal volume to dead space

choices.label: B
choices.text: The ratio of CO₂ production to O₂ consumption

choices.label: C
choices.text: The ratio of arterial to venous PO₂

choices.label: D
choices.text: The ratio of inspired to expired oxygen

choices.label: E
choices.text: The ratio of minute ventilation to alveolar ventilation

explanation.summary: The respiratory quotient (R or RQ) is the ratio of CO₂ production (V̇CO₂) to O₂ consumption (V̇O₂) during cellular metabolism. On a mixed diet, R is approximately 0.8, meaning 0.8 moles of CO₂ are produced for every 1 mole of O₂ consumed.
explanation.whyCorrect: R = V̇CO₂/V̇O₂. The normal value of 0.8 reflects a mixed diet. A pure carbohydrate diet gives R = 1.0 (equal CO₂ production and O₂ consumption), while a high-fat diet gives R closer to 0.7.

whyWrongByChoice.A: The ratio of tidal volume to dead space (Vt/VD) is not the respiratory quotient. It relates to ventilation efficiency, not metabolic gas exchange ratios.
whyWrongByChoice.C: The ratio of arterial to venous PO₂ reflects oxygen extraction by tissues, not the respiratory quotient.
whyWrongByChoice.D: The ratio of inspired to expired oxygen is not how R is defined. R specifically relates CO₂ production to O₂ consumption.
whyWrongByChoice.E: The ratio of minute to alveolar ventilation describes the proportion of effective ventilation but is not the respiratory quotient.

questions.stem: A patient who is hyperventilating has a PaCO₂ of 25 mmHg. Using the simplified alveolar gas equation (PAO₂ = 150 − PaCO₂/0.8), what is this patient's PAO₂?

explanation.summary: PAO₂ = 150 − (25/0.8) = 150 − 31.25 = 118.75 ≈ 119 mmHg. Hyperventilation lowers PaCO₂, which reduces the CO₂ dilution effect and raises PAO₂ above normal.
explanation.whyCorrect: When PaCO₂ drops due to hyperventilation, less CO₂ is present in the alveoli to displace oxygen. The alveolar gas equation shows: PAO₂ = 150 − (25/0.8) = 150 − 31.25 ≈ 119 mmHg, higher than the normal ~100 mmHg.

whyWrongByChoice.A: 100 mmHg is the normal PAO₂ with a PaCO₂ of 40 mmHg. With hyperventilation (PaCO₂ = 25), PAO₂ is elevated above 100.
whyWrongByChoice.B: 110 mmHg would correspond to a PaCO₂ of 32 mmHg: 150 − (32/0.8) = 110. The given PaCO₂ is 25.
whyWrongByChoice.D: 125 mmHg would correspond to a PaCO₂ of 20 mmHg: 150 − (20/0.8) = 125. The given PaCO₂ is 25.
whyWrongByChoice.E: 150 mmHg would require PaCO₂ of 0, meaning no CO₂ in the blood. This is physiologically impossible.

questions.stem: The normal A-a gradient (PAO₂ − PaO₂) exists even in healthy individuals. Which of the following best explains this normal gradient?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Incomplete diffusion equilibration across the alveolar membrane

choices.label: B
choices.text: Admixture of deoxygenated blood from bronchial and thebesian veins into the arterial supply

choices.label: C
choices.text: High airway resistance reducing oxygen delivery to alveoli

choices.label: D
choices.text: Oxygen consumption by the conducting airways

choices.label: E
choices.text: Uneven distribution of surfactant across alveoli

explanation.summary: The normal A-a gradient (~5-15 mmHg) exists because deoxygenated blood from the bronchial circulation and thebesian veins drains directly into the pulmonary veins or left heart, diluting the PaO₂ below PAO₂. This physiologic shunt is normal.
explanation.whyCorrect: Bronchial veins (supplying airway tissues) and thebesian veins (supplying the heart) return deoxygenated blood directly into the arterial system, creating a small right-to-left shunt. This mixing lowers PaO₂ by approximately 5-10 mmHg below PAO₂.

whyWrongByChoice.A: In healthy lungs, oxygen diffusion equilibration across the alveolar membrane is essentially complete during capillary transit. The A-a gradient is not due to diffusion limitation under normal conditions.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Airway resistance affects airflow but does not significantly reduce oxygen delivery to the alveoli in healthy individuals.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Oxygen consumption by conducting airways is negligible and does not significantly contribute to the A-a gradient.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Surfactant distribution does not significantly affect the A-a gradient. The gradient is due to physiologic venous admixture, not surfactant variations.

questions.stem: A mechanically ventilated patient develops an increasing VD/Vt ratio on the Bohr equation. This change most likely indicates which of the following?

explanation.summary: An increasing VD/Vt ratio means a greater proportion of each breath is wasted as dead space. This indicates worsening ventilation efficiency, potentially from increasing alveolar dead space due to worsening lung pathology or inappropriate ventilator settings.
explanation.whyCorrect: A rising VD/Vt indicates that more of each tidal volume is dead space ventilation. In a mechanically ventilated patient, this may signal worsening V/Q mismatch, pulmonary embolism, or inadequate ventilator settings, all leading to less efficient gas exchange.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Improving lung function would decrease VD/Vt as more alveoli participate effectively in gas exchange.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Decreasing metabolic rate affects CO₂ production but would not directly increase the VD/Vt ratio.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Improving gas exchange efficiency would be reflected by a decreasing VD/Vt ratio, not an increasing one.
whyWrongByChoice.E: A resolving PE would decrease alveolar dead space (restoring perfusion to previously blocked regions), decreasing VD/Vt.

questions.stem: Hyperbaric oxygen therapy delivers 100% oxygen at 2-3 times normal atmospheric pressure. In a patient with carbon monoxide poisoning, the primary benefit of this therapy is to:

choices.label: B
choices.text: Displace CO from hemoglobin by increasing dissolved O₂

explanation.summary: Hyperbaric oxygen therapy dramatically increases the partial pressure of dissolved O₂ in the blood. This high O₂ concentration competitively displaces CO from hemoglobin, restoring oxygen-carrying capacity. The freed CO is then eliminated through respiration.
explanation.whyCorrect: Carbon monoxide binds hemoglobin with ~200 times the affinity of oxygen. HBO therapy raises PaO₂ to very high levels (>1000 mmHg), increasing dissolved O₂ and accelerating CO displacement from carboxyhemoglobin. The high oxygen also maintains tissue oxygenation through dissolved O₂ alone.

whyWrongByChoice.A: HBO does not increase hemoglobin production. Its benefit is immediate — displacing CO from existing hemoglobin and increasing dissolved oxygen in plasma.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Reducing pulmonary vascular resistance is not the primary mechanism of benefit in CO poisoning. HBO therapy targets CO displacement from hemoglobin.
whyWrongByChoice.D: HBO therapy does not increase dead space. It increases dissolved oxygen in blood, improving tissue oxygenation.
whyWrongByChoice.E: HBO does not primarily work by decreasing respiratory rate. Its mechanism is through dramatically increased oxygen partial pressure.

questions.stem: In the alveolar gas equation, what happens to PAO₂ when a patient hypoventilates and PaCO₂ rises, assuming inspired oxygen remains constant?

explanation.summary: In the alveolar gas equation (PAO₂ = PiO₂ − PaCO₂/R), as PaCO₂ rises during hypoventilation, the subtracted term increases, causing PAO₂ to fall. More CO₂ in the alveoli displaces oxygen, reducing alveolar oxygen levels.
explanation.whyCorrect: Hypoventilation allows CO₂ to accumulate in the alveoli. Since the alveolar gas equation shows PAO₂ = PiO₂ − PaCO₂/R, a rising PaCO₂ directly decreases PAO₂. This is why hypoventilation causes both hypercapnia and hypoxemia.

whyWrongByChoice.A: PAO₂ increases with hyperventilation (lower PaCO₂), not hypoventilation. Hypoventilation raises PaCO₂ and decreases PAO₂.
whyWrongByChoice.B: PAO₂ is directly affected by PaCO₂ in the alveolar gas equation. It does not remain unchanged when PaCO₂ changes.
whyWrongByChoice.D: PAO₂ cannot become negative physiologically. Even with severe hypoventilation, PAO₂ remains positive (though dangerously low).
whyWrongByChoice.E: PAO₂ does not equal PaCO₂. They are related through the alveolar gas equation but have different values.

questions.stem: The partial pressure of oxygen in dry air at sea level is approximately 159 mmHg. How is this value calculated?

explanation.summary: The partial pressure of O₂ in dry air = FiO₂ × Patm = 0.21 × 760 = 159.6 mmHg. The water vapor correction (−47 mmHg) is applied only after air enters the humidified airways.
explanation.whyCorrect: In dry atmospheric air, the partial pressure of oxygen is simply the fraction of oxygen (21%) multiplied by total atmospheric pressure: PO₂ = 0.21 × 760 = 159.6 mmHg. The humidification correction produces PiO₂ = 149 mmHg, but this is after the air enters the airways.

whyWrongByChoice.A: This calculates PiO₂ (inspired oxygen after humidification in the airways), not the partial pressure in dry atmospheric air.
whyWrongByChoice.C: 0.78 × 760 calculates the partial pressure of nitrogen in air (since N₂ is ~78% of air), not oxygen.
whyWrongByChoice.D: 760 − 47 = 713 is the effective atmospheric pressure after water vapor correction, not the PO₂ of dry air.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Dividing atmospheric pressure by FiO₂ is not a meaningful calculation for partial pressure.

questions.stem: A mechanically ventilated patient has an endotracheal tube and ventilator circuit adding 50 mL to the conducting airway volume. This additional volume increases which type of dead space?

explanation.summary: Endotracheal tubes and ventilator circuits add volume to the conducting airway system, increasing anatomic dead space. This additional dead space must be accounted for when calculating tidal volume settings on the ventilator.
explanation.whyCorrect: The endotracheal tube and ventilator circuit extend the conducting airway pathway, adding to the volume of air that does not participate in gas exchange. This is an increase in anatomic dead space, and ventilator tidal volume settings must compensate for it.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Alveolar dead space refers to ventilated but unperfused alveoli. Ventilator tubing adds to the conducting airway volume, not alveolar dead space.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Functional residual capacity is the volume of air in the lungs at end-expiration. It is not affected by the external tubing of the ventilator circuit.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Tidal volume is the volume of air delivered per breath. While the ventilator's set tidal volume may need to be increased to compensate for added dead space, the tubing itself increases dead space, not tidal volume.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Inspiratory reserve volume is the additional air that can be inhaled beyond a normal tidal breath. It is not affected by ventilator tubing.

questions.stem: In a healthy individual, the normal VD/Vt ratio (dead space to tidal volume ratio) is approximately:

explanation.summary: In healthy individuals, the VD/Vt ratio is normally 20-40%, meaning 20-40% of each breath is dead space ventilation. This indicates efficient gas exchange with the majority of each breath reaching the alveoli.
explanation.whyCorrect: With a typical tidal volume of 500 mL and anatomic dead space of ~150 mL, VD/Vt ≈ 150/500 = 0.30 (30%). In healthy people, alveolar dead space is minimal, so VD/Vt falls in the 20-40% range.

whyWrongByChoice.A: 5-10% would mean nearly all inspired air reaches alveoli. This is physiologically impossible given the volume of the conducting airways.
whyWrongByChoice.C: 50-60% would indicate significant dead space ventilation, suggesting pathology such as pulmonary embolism or emphysema.
whyWrongByChoice.D: 70-80% would represent severely inefficient ventilation with most air wasted. This would cause hypercapnia and respiratory failure.
whyWrongByChoice.E: 90-100% would mean virtually no air reaches the alveoli, which is incompatible with life.

questions.stem: A patient at high altitude hyperventilates as a compensatory response. How does hyperventilation affect alveolar partial pressures of O₂ and CO₂?

explanation.summary: Hyperventilation increases alveolar ventilation, bringing in more fresh oxygen and blowing off more CO₂. This raises PAO₂ and lowers PACO₂. At altitude, this is a compensatory mechanism to partially offset the reduced PiO₂.
explanation.whyCorrect: Hyperventilation increases the delivery of fresh (oxygen-rich) air to the alveoli while simultaneously increasing the removal of CO₂. Per the alveolar gas equation, lower PACO₂ allows PAO₂ to rise, partially compensating for the lower PiO₂ at altitude.

whyWrongByChoice.A: This describes hypoventilation, not hyperventilation. Hypoventilation retains CO₂ and reduces O₂ delivery.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Hyperventilation increases PAO₂ but decreases PACO₂. They do not both increase.
whyWrongByChoice.D: PAO₂ increases with hyperventilation, not decreases. PACO₂ decreases as expected.
whyWrongByChoice.E: PAO₂ does change with hyperventilation. As PACO₂ falls, the alveolar gas equation predicts that PAO₂ rises.

questions.stem: In the Bohr equation, PECO₂ (mixed expired CO₂) is always lower than PaCO₂ (arterial CO₂). Which of the following explains this difference?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Expired air contains oxygen that dilutes CO₂

choices.label: B
choices.text: Dead space air containing no CO₂ dilutes the CO₂-rich alveolar air during expiration

choices.label: C
choices.text: CO₂ is absorbed by the airway mucosa during exhalation

choices.label: D
choices.text: Hemoglobin retains some CO₂ that is not exhaled

choices.label: E
choices.text: The respiratory quotient reduces the amount of CO₂ exhaled

explanation.summary: During exhalation, the first air expelled comes from the dead space and contains almost no CO₂ (it is essentially fresh atmospheric air). This CO₂-free dead space air dilutes the CO₂-rich air from the alveoli, making the mixed expired CO₂ (PECO₂) lower than the alveolar (and arterial) CO₂.
explanation.whyCorrect: The Bohr equation is based on the principle that dead space air (with negligible CO₂) mixes with alveolar air (CO₂-rich) during expiration. The greater the dead space, the more dilution occurs, and the larger the gap between PaCO₂ and PECO₂.

whyWrongByChoice.A: While expired air does contain oxygen, the reason PECO₂ is lower than PaCO₂ is specifically the dilution by CO₂-free dead space air, not by oxygen.
whyWrongByChoice.C: The airway mucosa does not absorb significant amounts of CO₂ during exhalation. CO₂ diffusion is from blood into alveoli, not from air into mucosa.
whyWrongByChoice.D: While some CO₂ remains bound to hemoglobin (as carbaminohemoglobin), this does not explain why PECO₂ is lower than PaCO₂. The dilution effect of dead space air is the primary reason.
whyWrongByChoice.E: The respiratory quotient relates CO₂ production to O₂ consumption. It does not explain why mixed expired CO₂ is lower than arterial CO₂.

questions.stem: A 55-year-old male with COPD has an arterial blood gas showing PaO₂ of 60 mmHg and PaCO₂ of 50 mmHg. His elevated PaCO₂ and decreased PaO₂ together indicate which of the following?

choices.label: C
choices.text: Impaired alveolar ventilation with increased dead space

explanation.summary: In COPD, airflow obstruction and alveolar destruction lead to inadequate alveolar ventilation and increased dead space. The elevated PaCO₂ reflects inadequate CO₂ removal, and the decreased PaO₂ reflects impaired oxygen uptake — both consistent with ventilatory defects characteristic of COPD.
explanation.whyCorrect: The combination of hypercapnia (PaCO₂ = 50) and hypoxemia (PaO₂ = 60) indicates that alveolar ventilation is insufficient relative to metabolic demands. In COPD, air trapping, airway obstruction, and alveolar destruction increase dead space and reduce effective gas exchange, producing both findings simultaneously.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Normal gas exchange would show PaO₂ of 80-100 mmHg and PaCO₂ of 35-45 mmHg. Both values are abnormal in this patient.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Isolated diffusion impairment (as in early fibrosis) typically shows hypoxemia with normal or low PaCO₂ (compensatory hyperventilation). Elevated PaCO₂ indicates a ventilatory problem.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Hyperventilation would produce low PaCO₂ (<35 mmHg) and respiratory alkalosis. This patient has elevated PaCO₂, indicating hypoventilation.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Metabolic acidosis with respiratory compensation would show low bicarbonate with compensatory low PaCO₂. This patient's elevated PaCO₂ indicates a primary respiratory problem, not compensation.

questions.stem: A 55-year-old man with COPD has the following arterial blood gas results on room air: PaO₂ 60 mmHg, PaCO₂ 50 mmHg, pH 7.30, HCO₃⁻ 29 mEq/L. His calculated A-a gradient is 27.5 mmHg (expected normal ~18 mmHg for his age). The elevated A-a gradient indicates which of the following?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Hypoventilation as the sole cause of hypoxemia

choices.label: B
choices.text: Impaired gas exchange at the alveolar-capillary level

choices.label: C
choices.text: Normal diffusion with low inspired oxygen

choices.label: E
choices.text: Adequate alveolar ventilation with increased dead space

explanation.summary: An elevated A-a gradient (above the age-adjusted normal) indicates impaired oxygen transfer from the alveoli into the blood. This suggests an intrapulmonary cause of hypoxemia such as V/Q mismatch, diffusion impairment, or shunting — not simply hypoventilation or low inspired O₂, which produce a normal A-a gradient.
explanation.whyCorrect: The A-a gradient measures the efficiency of oxygen transfer from alveoli to arterial blood. A normal gradient means hypoxemia is due to extrapulmonary causes (hypoventilation, low FiO₂). An elevated gradient pinpoints the problem to the lung itself — impaired diffusion, V/Q mismatch, or shunting.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Pure hypoventilation causes both PAO₂ and PaO₂ to decrease proportionally, maintaining a normal A-a gradient. This patient's elevated gradient indicates an additional intrapulmonary defect.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Low inspired oxygen (e.g., high altitude) reduces PAO₂ and PaO₂ equally, keeping the A-a gradient normal. An elevated gradient rules out this as the sole cause.
whyWrongByChoice.D: The ABG shows respiratory acidosis (pH 7.30, elevated PaCO₂) with metabolic compensation (elevated HCO₃⁻), not respiratory alkalosis.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Increased dead space without impaired gas exchange would show a normal A-a gradient. The elevated gradient here indicates a problem with oxygen transfer at the alveolar-capillary level.

questions.stem: The DLCO (diffusion capacity of the lung for carbon monoxide) test is used clinically to evaluate gas exchange. Carbon monoxide is used for this test because its exchange across the alveolar membrane is:

choices.label: B
choices.text: Diffusion-limited, due to its high affinity for hemoglobin

choices.label: E
choices.text: Equal in rate to oxygen diffusion

explanation.summary: CO binds hemoglobin with over 200 times the affinity of O₂, preventing buildup of dissolved CO partial pressure in plasma. This maintains a large, sustained pressure gradient, making CO exchange diffusion-limited — its transfer depends entirely on membrane properties, making it ideal for measuring diffusion capacity.
explanation.whyCorrect: Because CO binds avidly to hemoglobin, its partial pressure in capillary blood remains near zero throughout transit. The persistent alveolar-to-blood gradient means CO uptake is limited only by the diffusion properties of the membrane, not by blood flow. This makes it the ideal gas for measuring DLCO.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Oxygen at rest is perfusion-limited (equilibrates within 0.25 seconds of the 0.75-second transit time). CO, unlike O₂, never equilibrates due to its high hemoglobin affinity, making it diffusion-limited.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Nitrogen is an inert gas and does not participate significantly in diffusion across the alveolar membrane under normal conditions. There is no concept of "concentration-limited" exchange.
whyWrongByChoice.D: CO transfer is entirely dependent on membrane properties (thickness, surface area). That is precisely why it is used to assess diffusion capacity.
whyWrongByChoice.E: CO and O₂ have different diffusion characteristics. CO's uniquely high hemoglobin affinity makes it diffusion-limited, whereas O₂ is typically perfusion-limited.

questions.stem: Under normal resting conditions, oxygen equilibrates between alveolar gas and pulmonary capillary blood within approximately 0.25 seconds of the 0.75-second capillary transit time. This means oxygen exchange is normally:

explanation.summary: At rest, oxygen rapidly equilibrates across the alveolar membrane within the first third of capillary transit time. After equilibration, no further diffusion occurs because the pressure gradient is eliminated. Additional oxygen uptake can only increase with more blood flow (perfusion), making O₂ exchange perfusion-limited.
explanation.whyCorrect: Once capillary PO₂ equilibrates with alveolar PO₂ (~100 mmHg) at about 0.25 seconds, the pressure gradient disappears and diffusion stops. The remaining 0.50 seconds of transit provides a safety margin. To increase total O₂ uptake, more blood must flow through — hence perfusion-limited.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Oxygen is diffusion-limited only in disease states (thickened membrane, reduced surface area) or extreme exercise. Under normal resting conditions, it is perfusion-limited.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Ventilation-limited is not a standard classification of gas exchange. Gas exchange at the membrane level is classified as diffusion- or perfusion-limited.
whyWrongByChoice.D: While hemoglobin is essential for O₂ transport, the rate of equilibration across the membrane — not hemoglobin binding — determines whether exchange is perfusion- or diffusion-limited.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Resistance-limited is not a standard classification for gas exchange kinetics across the alveolar membrane.

questions.stem: A patient with interstitial lung disease develops exercise-induced hypoxemia despite normal resting PaO₂. The mechanism of this exercise-induced desaturation is best explained by:

choices.label: A
choices.text: Increased dead space ventilation at rest

choices.label: B
choices.text: Reduced capillary transit time preventing complete O₂ equilibration across the thickened membrane

choices.label: D
choices.text: Increased surfactant breakdown from physical exertion

explanation.summary: In interstitial lung disease, the alveolar membrane is thickened, slowing O₂ diffusion. At rest, the 0.75-second capillary transit time is still sufficient for equilibration. During exercise, increased cardiac output shortens transit time, and the thickened membrane cannot achieve equilibration, causing O₂ exchange to become diffusion-limited and resulting in hypoxemia.
explanation.whyCorrect: The thickened membrane delays O₂ equilibration. At rest, there is still enough transit time for near-complete equilibration. During exercise, increased blood flow shortens capillary transit time (< 0.25 seconds may be needed for diffusion), and the combination of thickened membrane and shorter transit time unmasks the diffusion limitation.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Dead space ventilation does not typically increase with exercise. Exercise-induced hypoxemia in ILD is due to diffusion limitation, not dead space.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Hemoglobin concentration does not decrease during exercise. While hemoglobin is important for O₂ carrying, the mechanism here is impaired diffusion across the thickened membrane.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Surfactant is not broken down by physical exertion. Surfactant deficiency is not a feature of exercise-induced hypoxemia in ILD.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Bronchospasm causes exercise-induced asthma, which is a separate condition. ILD exercise-induced hypoxemia results from impaired diffusion across a thickened membrane, not airway obstruction.

questions.stem: A patient with a hemoglobin of 7 g/dL (normal 12-16 g/dL) undergoes pulmonary function testing. How would severe anemia be expected to affect the DLCO?

explanation.summary: DLCO measures gas transfer from alveoli into pulmonary capillary blood, including uptake by hemoglobin in red blood cells. In anemia, reduced hemoglobin and RBC concentration decreases the blood's capacity to bind CO, resulting in a decreased DLCO.
explanation.whyCorrect: DLCO depends on both membrane diffusion and the volume of hemoglobin available to bind CO. In severe anemia, reduced hemoglobin means less CO is bound per unit time, lowering the measured DLCO even if the alveolar membrane is normal.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Increased DLCO is seen in conditions with increased pulmonary capillary blood volume (e.g., exercise) or polycythemia (increased hemoglobin). Anemia has the opposite effect.
whyWrongByChoice.C: DLCO is significantly affected by hemoglobin concentration. Anemia is a well-established cause of reduced DLCO.
whyWrongByChoice.D: DLCO can still be measured in anemia. It will be reduced, but the test remains valid and interpretable.
whyWrongByChoice.E: DLCO changes predictably with hemoglobin levels. It decreases proportionally with anemia and does not fluctuate unpredictably.

questions.stem: A healthy 25-year-old medical student has an A-a gradient of 8 mmHg. Which of the following is the normal physiologic reason for this gradient being greater than zero?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Incomplete diffusion equilibration in healthy lungs

choices.label: B
choices.text: Venous admixture from bronchial and thebesian veins

choices.label: D
choices.text: Dead space ventilation diluting arterial blood

choices.label: E
choices.text: Hemoglobin's inability to fully saturate at normal PaO₂

explanation.summary: In healthy lungs, oxygen diffusion equilibrates completely during capillary transit. The normal A-a gradient exists because deoxygenated blood from bronchial veins (supplying airway tissue) and thebesian veins (supplying myocardium) drains into the oxygenated arterial supply, slightly reducing PaO₂ below PAO₂.
explanation.whyCorrect: The physiologic shunt from bronchial and thebesian veins adds a small amount of deoxygenated blood to the oxygenated pulmonary venous return. This venous admixture lowers PaO₂ by approximately 5-10 mmHg below PAO₂, creating the normal A-a gradient.

whyWrongByChoice.A: In healthy lungs, O₂ equilibrates completely within the first third of the 0.75-second capillary transit time. Incomplete equilibration occurs only in disease.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Surfactant reduces surface tension in alveoli. It does not absorb oxygen or contribute to the A-a gradient.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Dead space ventilation affects the efficiency of CO₂ removal and alveolar ventilation but does not directly cause the A-a gradient. The gradient reflects venous admixture.
whyWrongByChoice.E: At a normal PaO₂ of ~95 mmHg, hemoglobin is approximately 97% saturated. While not 100%, this is not the cause of the A-a gradient.

questions.stem: A patient with hypoxemia has a PaO₂ of 55 mmHg and PaCO₂ of 55 mmHg. The A-a gradient is calculated to be normal. Which of the following is the most likely cause of this patient's hypoxemia?

explanation.summary: A normal A-a gradient with hypoxemia indicates an extrapulmonary cause. The elevated PaCO₂ (55 mmHg) confirms hypoventilation, where inadequate alveolar ventilation reduces both PAO₂ and PaO₂ proportionally, maintaining a normal A-a gradient.
explanation.whyCorrect: In hypoventilation, CO₂ accumulates and O₂ decreases in the alveoli per the alveolar gas equation. Both PAO₂ and PaO₂ fall together because the lung's gas exchange capacity is intact — hence a normal A-a gradient. The high PaCO₂ confirms the ventilatory failure.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Pulmonary embolism creates alveolar dead space and V/Q mismatch, which would elevate the A-a gradient.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Pneumonia with V/Q mismatch impairs gas exchange at the alveolar level, elevating the A-a gradient.
whyWrongByChoice.D: An intracardiac shunt bypasses the lungs, mixing deoxygenated blood into the arterial supply and elevating the A-a gradient.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Pulmonary fibrosis thickens the alveolar membrane, impairing diffusion and elevating the A-a gradient.

questions.stem: The respiratory membrane (alveolar-capillary barrier) is approximately 0.5 μm thick. The distance between the alveolar lumen and the capillary lumen is minimized for which primary purpose?

choices.label: B
choices.text: To maximize the rate of gas diffusion

choices.label: C
choices.text: To prevent surfactant from entering the blood

choices.label: D
choices.text: To maintain a high colloid osmotic pressure

choices.label: E
choices.text: To reduce the need for hemoglobin

explanation.summary: Per Fick's law, diffusion rate is inversely proportional to membrane thickness. The extremely thin alveolar-capillary barrier (~0.5 μm) minimizes the diffusion distance for O₂ and CO₂, maximizing the rate of gas exchange between alveolar air and capillary blood.
explanation.whyCorrect: A thinner membrane allows faster diffusion because gas molecules traverse a shorter distance. The 0.5 μm barrier represents the evolutionary optimization for rapid gas exchange while still maintaining structural integrity.

whyWrongByChoice.A: The thinness of the alveolar membrane has no relationship to airway resistance, which is determined by airway diameter in the conducting zone.
whyWrongByChoice.C: The barrier prevents fluid and large molecules from crossing, but its extreme thinness is primarily an adaptation for gas exchange efficiency, not surfactant containment.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Colloid osmotic pressure is determined by plasma protein concentration, not by membrane thickness.
whyWrongByChoice.E: The thin membrane does not reduce the need for hemoglobin. Hemoglobin is essential for oxygen transport regardless of membrane thickness.

questions.stem: A patient with emphysema has a decreased DLCO. Recent evidence suggests that the most important mechanism reducing DLCO in emphysema is:

choices.label: B
choices.text: Decreased volume of red blood cells in pulmonary capillaries

explanation.summary: While older textbooks attributed low DLCO in emphysema primarily to loss of surface area, more recent evidence suggests that most conditions reducing DLCO do so primarily by decreasing the volume of red blood cells in pulmonary capillaries (from destruction of alveolar capillary beds).
explanation.whyCorrect: Emphysema destroys alveolar walls and their associated capillary networks. This reduces the total volume of RBCs available in pulmonary capillaries to bind CO during the DLCO test. The reduced capillary blood volume is now considered the primary mechanism of decreased DLCO in emphysema.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Increased membrane thickness characterizes fibrosis, not emphysema. In emphysema, alveolar walls are destroyed rather than thickened.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Airway resistance is increased in emphysema due to loss of elastic recoil and airway collapse, but this does not directly affect DLCO measurement.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Surfactant deficiency is not a feature of emphysema. The DLCO reduction is due to loss of alveolar capillary beds.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Bronchial smooth muscle hypertrophy is more characteristic of chronic bronchitis and asthma. It does not explain the decreased DLCO in emphysema.

questions.stem: Normal arterial blood gas values for PaO₂ and PaCO₂ are, respectively:

explanation.summary: Normal ABG reference values are PaO₂: 75-105 mmHg (indicating adequate oxygen diffusion across the alveolar-capillary membrane) and PaCO₂: 33-45 mmHg (reflecting efficient ventilation and CO₂ removal).
explanation.whyCorrect: Per NBME reference values, normal PaO₂ is 75-105 mmHg and PaCO₂ is 33-45 mmHg. Values outside these ranges indicate impaired oxygenation (hypoxemia) or ventilation (hypo- or hypercapnia), respectively.

whyWrongByChoice.A: A PaO₂ of 40-60 mmHg indicates hypoxemia, and a PaCO₂ of 55-65 mmHg indicates hypercapnia. These are abnormal values.
whyWrongByChoice.C: A PaO₂ of 100-120 mmHg might be seen with supplemental oxygen, and a PaCO₂ of 20-30 mmHg would indicate severe hyperventilation.
whyWrongByChoice.D: A PaO₂ of 60-80 mmHg is borderline to mildly hypoxemic, and a PaCO₂ of 45-55 mmHg indicates mild hypercapnia.
whyWrongByChoice.E: A PaO₂ of 50-70 mmHg indicates hypoxemia. While the PaCO₂ range partially overlaps normal, the PaO₂ range is abnormal.

questions.stem: The L/S ratio is used to assess fetal lung maturity. An L/S ratio of ≥ 2.0 indicates adequate surfactant levels and is typically achieved at approximately which gestational age?

explanation.summary: The lecithin-to-sphingomyelin (L/S) ratio reaches ≥ 2.0 at approximately 34-36 weeks' gestation, indicating adequate surfactant production for independent breathing. Infants born before this point are at risk for NRDS.
explanation.whyCorrect: Surfactant production increases progressively throughout gestation, and the L/S ratio reaches the threshold of 2.0 at approximately 34-36 weeks. This is the benchmark for fetal lung maturity and guides clinical decisions about antenatal corticosteroid administration.

whyWrongByChoice.A: At 20-22 weeks, surfactant production has just begun and is in very small amounts. The L/S ratio is well below 2.0.
whyWrongByChoice.B: At 26-28 weeks, surfactant production is increasing but still insufficient for an L/S ratio of 2.0.
whyWrongByChoice.C: At 30-32 weeks, surfactant levels are rising but typically have not reached the 2.0 threshold.
whyWrongByChoice.E: 38-40 weeks is full term. While the L/S ratio is well above 2.0 at this point, adequate levels are typically reached earlier, at 34-36 weeks.

questions.stem: A 30-year-old woman with polycythemia vera undergoes pulmonary function testing. Which of the following changes in DLCO would be expected?

choices.label: D
choices.text: DLCO cannot be measured in polycythemia

explanation.summary: In polycythemia, increased RBC concentration and hemoglobin in pulmonary capillaries enhances the blood's capacity to bind CO, resulting in an increased DLCO on pulmonary function testing.
explanation.whyCorrect: DLCO depends on both membrane diffusion and the volume of hemoglobin available to bind CO. Polycythemia increases the RBC mass and hemoglobin concentration, providing more binding sites for CO and increasing the measured DLCO.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Decreased DLCO occurs in conditions that reduce hemoglobin (anemia) or capillary blood volume (emphysema). Polycythemia increases hemoglobin, raising DLCO.
whyWrongByChoice.C: DLCO is not normal in polycythemia — the increased hemoglobin concentration significantly enhances CO uptake.
whyWrongByChoice.D: DLCO can be measured in polycythemia. The elevated hemoglobin actually makes the test more sensitive.
whyWrongByChoice.E: DLCO remains elevated as long as polycythemia persists. There is no normalization pattern without treatment of the underlying condition.

questions.stem: The age-adjusted normal A-a gradient can be estimated using which formula?

explanation.summary: The age-adjusted normal A-a gradient is estimated using the formula (Age/4) + 4. For a 20-year-old, this gives ~9 mmHg; for a 60-year-old, ~19 mmHg. Values above this suggest impaired gas exchange.
explanation.whyCorrect: The formula (Age/4) + 4 provides a quick estimate of the upper limit of normal for the A-a gradient. The A-a gradient increases with age due to progressive loss of alveolar surface area and ventilation-perfusion mismatch.

whyWrongByChoice.A: (Age × 4) + 4 would give unrealistically large values (e.g., 164 for a 40-year-old). The correct formula divides age by 4.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Age − 10 would give negative values for young patients and does not reflect the actual age-related change in A-a gradient.
whyWrongByChoice.D: 150 − (Age/2) is not a formula for the A-a gradient. It does not correspond to any standard clinical calculation.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Age + 10 would overestimate the normal A-a gradient for all age groups.

questions.stem: Two processes maintain the partial pressure gradients at the alveolar-capillary interface that drive gas diffusion. These two processes are:

choices.label: C
choices.text: Hemoglobin binding and carbonic anhydrase activity

explanation.summary: Partial pressure gradients at the alveolar-capillary interface are maintained by continuous ventilation (which refreshes alveolar air with oxygen and removes CO₂) and continuous perfusion (which brings deoxygenated, CO₂-rich blood and removes oxygenated blood).
explanation.whyCorrect: Without continuous ventilation, alveolar O₂ would be depleted and CO₂ would accumulate. Without continuous perfusion, capillary blood would equilibrate with alveolar gas and the gradient would disappear. Both processes are needed to sustain the driving force for diffusion.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Surfactant prevents alveolar collapse and mucociliary clearance removes particles. Neither directly maintains the partial pressure gradients for gas exchange.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Hemoglobin binding and carbonic anhydrase facilitate O₂ and CO₂ transport within the blood but do not maintain the alveolar-to-blood pressure gradients themselves.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Lymphatic drainage removes excess fluid and bronchial circulation supplies airway tissue. Neither maintains the gas exchange gradients at the alveolar-capillary interface.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Gas exchange across the alveolar membrane occurs by simple passive diffusion, not active transport or facilitated diffusion.

questions.stem: A 60-year-old man with pulmonary fibrosis has the following ABG: PaO₂ 62 mmHg, PaCO₂ 32 mmHg. The low PaCO₂ in the setting of hypoxemia is best explained by:

choices.label: A
choices.text: Diffusion impairment affecting CO₂ more than O₂

choices.label: D
choices.text: Decreased CO₂ production from tissue hypoxia

choices.label: E
choices.text: Increased dead space selectively trapping CO₂

explanation.summary: In pulmonary fibrosis, the thickened alveolar membrane impairs O₂ diffusion (larger molecule, lower solubility) more than CO₂ diffusion (20x more soluble). The resulting hypoxemia stimulates peripheral chemoreceptors, triggering compensatory hyperventilation that lowers PaCO₂ below normal.
explanation.whyCorrect: Hypoxemia stimulates the carotid body chemoreceptors, increasing ventilatory drive. The compensatory hyperventilation effectively blows off CO₂ (since CO₂ diffusion is 20x more efficient than O₂), lowering PaCO₂ despite the impaired O₂ transfer.

whyWrongByChoice.A: CO₂ is 20 times more soluble than O₂ and diffuses much more readily. Diffusion impairment affects O₂ transfer more than CO₂ transfer.
whyWrongByChoice.C: The low PaCO₂ with hypoxemia in this context is a respiratory compensation (hyperventilation), not a metabolic process.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Tissue hypoxia does not significantly decrease CO₂ production. Cellular metabolism continues and CO₂ production remains relatively constant.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Dead space does not selectively trap CO₂. Increased dead space would impair both O₂ and CO₂ exchange and would lead to increased (not decreased) PaCO₂.

questions.stem: Nitrous oxide (N₂O) is used as an example of perfusion-limited gas exchange. Which property of N₂O makes it perfusion-limited?

choices.label: A
choices.text: It has a very low solubility in blood

choices.label: B
choices.text: It diffuses rapidly and does not bind hemoglobin, so its partial pressure equilibrates almost instantly

choices.label: C
choices.text: It binds hemoglobin with high affinity

choices.label: D
choices.text: It has a large molecular weight that slows diffusion

choices.label: E
choices.text: It is actively transported across the alveolar membrane

explanation.summary: N₂O diffuses rapidly across the alveolar membrane and does not bind hemoglobin. Its partial pressure in blood equilibrates with alveolar N₂O almost instantly. Once equilibrated, further uptake depends solely on blood flow (perfusion), making it the classic perfusion-limited gas.
explanation.whyCorrect: Because N₂O does not bind hemoglobin, its dissolved partial pressure in blood rises quickly to match alveolar partial pressure. The gradient disappears rapidly, and no more N₂O can diffuse unless fresh blood arrives — hence perfusion-limited.

whyWrongByChoice.A: N₂O actually has moderate solubility in blood and tissues (it is an anesthetic). Its perfusion limitation is because it does not bind hemoglobin, not because of low solubility.
whyWrongByChoice.C: N₂O does not bind hemoglobin. High hemoglobin affinity characterizes CO, which is diffusion-limited.
whyWrongByChoice.D: N₂O has a relatively small molecular weight (44 g/mol). Large molecular weight would slow diffusion, but N₂O diffuses rapidly.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Gas exchange across the alveolar membrane occurs by passive diffusion, not active transport. No respiratory gases are actively transported.

questions.stem: A patient with pneumonia has a PaO₂ of 58 mmHg on room air and a calculated A-a gradient of 35 mmHg. Which of the following best categorizes this patient's A-a gradient?

explanation.summary: An A-a gradient of 35 mmHg is significantly elevated (normal is <10-15 mmHg in young adults). In pneumonia, alveolar consolidation causes V/Q mismatch and intrapulmonary shunting, impairing oxygen transfer from alveoli to blood and elevating the A-a gradient.
explanation.whyCorrect: Pneumonia causes alveolar filling with inflammatory exudate, creating areas of V/Q mismatch (poorly ventilated but perfused alveoli) and shunt. This intrapulmonary pathology impairs oxygen transfer, evidenced by the markedly elevated A-a gradient.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Hypoventilation alone produces a normal A-a gradient because PAO₂ and PaO₂ decrease proportionally. An A-a gradient of 35 is abnormal.
whyWrongByChoice.B: High-altitude exposure decreases PAO₂ and PaO₂ equally, maintaining a normal A-a gradient. This patient's gradient is markedly elevated.
whyWrongByChoice.D: A decreased A-a gradient would suggest improved gas exchange. A gradient of 35 mmHg indicates significant impairment.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Metabolic alkalosis is a primary metabolic disturbance and does not directly cause an elevated A-a gradient. The elevated gradient here reflects intrapulmonary pathology.

questions.stem: The oxygen partial pressure gradient driving diffusion from the alveoli into the pulmonary capillary blood is approximately:

explanation.summary: PAO₂ is approximately 100 mmHg and mixed venous PO₂ entering the pulmonary capillary is approximately 40 mmHg. The difference (100 − 40 = 60 mmHg) is the gradient driving O₂ diffusion into the blood.
explanation.whyCorrect: The pressure gradient for oxygen diffusion is PAO₂ − PvO₂ = 100 − 40 = 60 mmHg. This substantial gradient allows rapid O₂ equilibration within the first third of capillary transit time under normal conditions.

whyWrongByChoice.A: 6 mmHg is the CO₂ gradient (PvCO₂ 46 − PACO₂ 40 = 6 mmHg), not the O₂ gradient.
whyWrongByChoice.B: 20 mmHg is not the correct O₂ gradient. The difference between alveolar and mixed venous PO₂ is 60 mmHg.
whyWrongByChoice.C: 40 mmHg is the mixed venous PO₂ (the lower end of the gradient), not the gradient itself.
whyWrongByChoice.E: 100 mmHg is the PAO₂ (the higher end of the gradient). The gradient is the difference between the two sides.

questions.stem: Under which of the following conditions can normally perfusion-limited oxygen exchange become diffusion-limited?

choices.label: B
choices.text: Thickened alveolar membrane combined with vigorous exercise

explanation.summary: When the alveolar membrane is thickened (e.g., fibrosis) and blood flow increases during exercise (shortening capillary transit time), there may not be enough time for O₂ to equilibrate across the abnormal membrane. This shifts O₂ from perfusion-limited to diffusion-limited exchange.
explanation.whyCorrect: A thickened membrane slows O₂ diffusion. During exercise, increased cardiac output shortens capillary transit time. The combination means O₂ cannot equilibrate during the shortened transit, and further uptake is limited by the rate of diffusion rather than blood flow.

whyWrongByChoice.A: At rest with normal lungs, O₂ equilibrates easily within the transit time. There is no diffusion limitation.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Supplemental O₂ increases the alveolar-to-blood pressure gradient, which would enhance diffusion, not limit it.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Increased hemoglobin provides more O₂ binding capacity. It would enhance O₂ uptake, not create a diffusion limitation.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Decreased metabolic rate reduces O₂ demand and CO₂ production. There is no stimulus to make O₂ exchange diffusion-limited during sleep.

questions.stem: A patient presents with cyanosis and labored breathing. An ABG on room air shows: pH 7.30, PaO₂ 60 mmHg, PaCO₂ 50 mmHg, HCO₃⁻ 24 mEq/L. Which acid-base disturbance is present?

explanation.summary: pH 7.30 indicates acidemia. PaCO₂ is elevated (50 mmHg, normal 33-45), indicating CO₂ retention from hypoventilation. HCO₃⁻ is normal (24 mEq/L, normal 22-28), meaning there is no metabolic compensation yet. This is an acute respiratory acidosis.
explanation.whyCorrect: The low pH with elevated PaCO₂ and normal HCO₃⁻ indicates uncompensated respiratory acidosis. The high PaCO₂ reflects inadequate alveolar ventilation, which is also consistent with the hypoxemia (PaO₂ 60 mmHg).

whyWrongByChoice.A: Metabolic acidosis would show low HCO₃⁻ with normal or low PaCO₂ (respiratory compensation). This patient's HCO₃⁻ is normal and PaCO₂ is elevated.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Metabolic alkalosis would show elevated pH and elevated HCO₃⁻. This patient has acidemia with normal HCO₃⁻.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Respiratory alkalosis would show elevated pH with low PaCO₂ from hyperventilation. This patient has the opposite pattern.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Mixed alkalosis would produce elevated pH. This patient has acidemia (pH 7.30).

questions.stem: Carbon monoxide binds hemoglobin with approximately how many times greater affinity than oxygen?

explanation.summary: Carbon monoxide binds hemoglobin with over 200 times the affinity of oxygen. This high affinity prevents CO partial pressure from rising in the plasma, maintaining a sustained diffusion gradient and making CO exchange diffusion-limited.
explanation.whyCorrect: CO's ~200x greater affinity for hemoglobin compared to O₂ is what makes it so dangerous in poisoning and so useful in the DLCO test. The avid binding keeps dissolved CO levels near zero, sustaining the alveolar-to-blood gradient throughout capillary transit.

whyWrongByChoice.A: 2 times would not produce the dramatic diffusion-limited behavior observed with CO. The affinity is orders of magnitude higher.
whyWrongByChoice.B: 20 times is the relative solubility advantage of CO₂ over O₂. CO's hemoglobin affinity is 200 times that of O₂.
whyWrongByChoice.C: 50 times underestimates CO's hemoglobin affinity. The accepted value is approximately 200-250 times.
whyWrongByChoice.E: 2,000 times overestimates the affinity. The widely cited value is approximately 200 times greater than oxygen.

questions.stem: An ABG performed on a patient receiving 100% oxygen by mechanical ventilation shows PaO₂ of 55 mmHg. The persistently low PaO₂ despite high FiO₂ most likely indicates which cause of hypoxemia?

choices.label: E
choices.text: V/Q mismatch that corrects with oxygen

explanation.summary: Right-to-left shunt causes blood to bypass the alveoli entirely. No amount of supplemental oxygen can oxygenate blood that never contacts ventilated alveoli. Persistent hypoxemia despite 100% FiO₂ is the hallmark of a true shunt.
explanation.whyCorrect: In a right-to-left shunt, deoxygenated blood passes directly from the right heart to the left heart (or through unventilated lung regions) without encountering alveolar gas. Since this shunted blood cannot be oxygenated regardless of FiO₂, PaO₂ remains low even with 100% oxygen.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Hypoventilation-induced hypoxemia readily corrects with supplemental oxygen, which increases the PAO₂ and overcomes the reduced ventilation.
whyWrongByChoice.B: High-altitude hypoxemia is due to reduced PiO₂ and would be completely corrected by administering 100% oxygen.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Diffusion impairment responds to supplemental oxygen because increasing PAO₂ steepens the pressure gradient across the thickened membrane.
whyWrongByChoice.E: V/Q mismatch typically responds to supplemental oxygen because increasing alveolar O₂ in poorly ventilated regions improves oxygenation of blood flowing through those areas.

questions.stem: Compared to the systemic circulation, the pulmonary circulation is characterized by which of the following hemodynamic properties?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Higher mean arterial pressure and higher vascular resistance

choices.label: B
choices.text: Lower mean arterial pressure and lower vascular resistance

choices.label: C
choices.text: Equal mean arterial pressure but lower cardiac output

choices.label: D
choices.text: Higher vascular resistance with thicker vessel walls

choices.label: E
choices.text: Lower cardiac output but higher blood volume

explanation.summary: The pulmonary circulation operates as a low-pressure, low-resistance system. Mean pulmonary arterial pressure is ~15 mmHg (vs ~95 mmHg systemic), and pulmonary vascular resistance is approximately one-tenth of systemic vascular resistance, despite handling the same cardiac output.
explanation.whyCorrect: The pulmonary circulation only needs to pump blood a short distance to the lungs (about 10 cm above the heart). The thin-walled, compliant vessels and short circuit result in low pressure and resistance, protecting the delicate alveolar capillaries from damage.

whyWrongByChoice.A: The systemic circulation has higher pressures and resistance, not the pulmonary. Pulmonary MAP is ~15 mmHg vs systemic ~95 mmHg.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Cardiac output is equal in both circulations — the same volume of blood that leaves the right heart passes through the pulmonary circulation and returns to the left heart.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Pulmonary vessels have thinner walls with less smooth muscle, corresponding to their lower resistance. Systemic arteries have thicker walls.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Cardiac output is the same in both circulations. The pulmonary circulation has lower pressure but does not have a different cardiac output.

questions.stem: In the upright lung, pulmonary blood flow is greatest at the base and lowest at the apex. This distribution is primarily due to which of the following?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Higher surfactant concentration at the base

choices.label: D
choices.text: Greater number of alveoli at the base

choices.label: E
choices.text: Higher oxygen consumption at the base

explanation.summary: In the upright position, gravity creates a hydrostatic pressure gradient within the pulmonary vasculature. Blood flow is highest at the base because gravity increases hydrostatic pressure, making perfusion easier. At the apex, reduced hydrostatic pressure makes it harder for the low-pressure right heart to perfuse these regions.
explanation.whyCorrect: The right ventricle generates low pressures (~15 mmHg mean PA pressure). Gravity adds hydrostatic pressure at the base (increasing perfusion) and subtracts it at the apex (decreasing perfusion), creating the characteristic gradient of blood flow distribution.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Surfactant distribution is relatively uniform and does not determine regional blood flow distribution.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Sympathetic vasoconstriction is not the mechanism for apex-base flow differences. The gradient is due to passive gravitational effects on hydrostatic pressure.
whyWrongByChoice.D: The number of alveoli is relatively similar throughout the lung. Blood flow distribution is determined by pressure gradients, not alveolar density.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Regional oxygen consumption does not drive the apex-to-base perfusion gradient. The distribution is gravity-dependent.

questions.stem: West's Zone 1, where alveolar pressure exceeds pulmonary arterial pressure and there is no blood flow, is generally absent in healthy individuals. Under which of the following conditions might Zone 1 develop?

choices.label: B
choices.text: Positive pressure mechanical ventilation or hypovolemic shock

explanation.summary: Zone 1 occurs when alveolar pressure exceeds arterial pressure (PA > Pa), collapsing capillaries and eliminating flow. This can develop during positive pressure ventilation (which increases alveolar pressure) or hypovolemic shock (which decreases arterial pressure).
explanation.whyCorrect: Positive pressure ventilation raises alveolar pressure, which can exceed the already-low pulmonary arterial pressure, especially at the apex. Similarly, hypovolemic shock reduces cardiac output and arterial pressure, allowing alveolar pressure to predominate and create Zone 1 conditions.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Exercise increases cardiac output and pulmonary arterial pressure, which would expand Zone 3 and recruit previously unperfused capillaries — the opposite of Zone 1 development.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Chronic hypoxemia from COPD causes hypoxic vasoconstriction and pulmonary hypertension, which increases arterial pressure, not the alveolar-exceeds-arterial condition of Zone 1.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Left ventricular heart failure increases pulmonary venous pressure and congestion, expanding Zone 3 conditions rather than creating Zone 1.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Supine positioning reduces the vertical height of the lung, making blood flow more uniform and less likely to create Zone 1 conditions.

questions.stem: A patient with COPD develops chronic hypoxemia. The pulmonary vascular response to chronic alveolar hypoxia is:

choices.label: A
choices.text: Vasodilation to increase blood flow to hypoxic areas

choices.label: B
choices.text: Vasoconstriction that diverts blood from hypoxic areas

explanation.summary: Hypoxic pulmonary vasoconstriction (HPV) is a unique response where pulmonary arterioles constrict in response to low alveolar PO₂. This diverts blood away from poorly ventilated lung regions toward better-oxygenated areas, optimizing V/Q matching.
explanation.whyCorrect: Unlike systemic vessels that dilate in hypoxia, pulmonary vessels constrict. HPV begins within seconds of hypoxia exposure and redirects blood flow from poorly ventilated regions to better-ventilated areas, maximizing overall blood oxygenation.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Vasodilation in response to hypoxia is the systemic vascular response. Pulmonary vessels do the opposite — they constrict to redirect flow away from hypoxic areas.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Pulmonary vessels actively respond to alveolar hypoxia. HPV is a well-established, rapid response that significantly alters regional blood flow.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Lymphatic drainage is important for fluid balance but is not the primary vascular response to hypoxia.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Surfactant production is not directly affected by acute hypoxia in this context. The primary response is vascular constriction.

questions.stem: Chronic global hypoxic pulmonary vasoconstriction, as seen in severe COPD, can lead to which of the following cardiac complications?

explanation.summary: When hypoxia is global (affecting the entire lung), widespread HPV increases pulmonary vascular resistance throughout the lungs, causing pulmonary hypertension. The right ventricle must work harder to pump against this elevated resistance, leading to right ventricular hypertrophy (cor pulmonale).
explanation.whyCorrect: Chronic widespread HPV in severe COPD elevates pulmonary arterial pressure (pulmonary hypertension). The right ventricle hypertrophies to compensate for the increased afterload, a condition called cor pulmonale. If the right ventricle eventually fails, right-sided heart failure ensues.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Left ventricular hypertrophy results from systemic hypertension or aortic stenosis — conditions that increase left ventricular afterload. Pulmonary hypertension affects the right ventricle.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Mitral valve stenosis is typically caused by rheumatic heart disease. It is not caused by HPV or pulmonary hypertension.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Aortic dissection is caused by hypertension and connective tissue disorders affecting the aorta, not by pulmonary vascular changes.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Left atrial dilation is seen in left heart disease (mitral valve disease, LV failure). Pulmonary hypertension from HPV primarily affects the right heart.

questions.stem: During vigorous exercise, cardiac output increases from 5 L/min to 25 L/min. Pulmonary vascular resistance decreases despite the increased flow. Which mechanisms allow the pulmonary circulation to accommodate this increased output?

choices.label: B
choices.text: Recruitment and distension of pulmonary capillaries

choices.label: E
choices.text: Activation of the Frank-Starling mechanism in the lung

explanation.summary: The pulmonary circulation accommodates increased cardiac output through two mechanisms: recruitment (opening of previously unperfused capillaries) and distension (expansion of already-perfused vessels). Per Poiseuille's law, even small increases in vessel diameter significantly reduce resistance.
explanation.whyCorrect: With increased cardiac output, higher pulmonary arterial pressure opens previously collapsed capillaries (recruitment) and widens already-open vessels (distension). These mechanisms increase the total cross-sectional area of the vascular bed, reducing PVR and allowing a 5-fold increase in flow with minimal pressure rise.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Sympathetic vasoconstriction would increase resistance, not decrease it. The pulmonary circulation's response is passive vessel opening, not active constriction.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Surfactant production is unrelated to pulmonary vascular resistance changes during exercise.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Pulmonary veins do not have valves. This is not a mechanism for accommodating increased flow.
whyWrongByChoice.E: The Frank-Starling mechanism applies to cardiac muscle (increased preload → increased stroke volume), not to the pulmonary vascular bed directly.

questions.stem: Pulmonary vascular resistance (PVR) varies with lung volume in a U-shaped curve. At which lung volume is PVR at its minimum?

explanation.summary: PVR follows a U-shaped curve with lung volume, reaching its minimum at functional residual capacity (FRC). At residual volume, increased pleural pressures compress vessels. At total lung capacity, overdistended alveoli compress capillaries. FRC represents the optimal balance.
explanation.whyCorrect: At FRC (end of normal expiration), the opposing effects on alveolar and extra-alveolar vessels are balanced, minimizing total PVR. Below FRC, compressed extra-alveolar vessels increase resistance. Above FRC, expanded alveoli compress adjacent capillaries, also increasing resistance.

whyWrongByChoice.A: At residual volume, the lungs are maximally deflated. Increased pleural pressures compress extra-alveolar vessels, raising PVR.
whyWrongByChoice.C: At total lung capacity, maximally inflated alveoli compress the surrounding capillaries, increasing PVR.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Inspiratory capacity is the maximum air that can be inhaled from FRC. PVR at this volume would be increasing as alveoli expand and compress capillaries.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Tidal volume is the amount of air breathed in a normal cycle. It is not a static lung volume at which PVR can be uniquely characterized.

questions.stem: Tuberculosis tends to reactivate in the apex of the lungs. Which feature of the apex creates a favorable environment for Mycobacterium tuberculosis?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Low oxygen tension from poor ventilation

choices.label: B
choices.text: High ventilation-to-perfusion ratio creating an oxygen-rich environment

choices.label: C
choices.text: Thick alveolar membranes that protect bacteria from immune cells

choices.label: D
choices.text: High blood flow bringing nutrients to the bacteria

choices.label: E
choices.text: Absence of alveolar macrophages at the apex

explanation.summary: At the lung apex, blood flow is minimal (due to gravity) while ventilation is relatively maintained, creating a high V/Q ratio. This results in high alveolar PO₂, providing an oxygen-rich environment that favors the growth of Mycobacterium tuberculosis, an obligate aerobe.
explanation.whyCorrect: M. tuberculosis thrives in high-oxygen environments. The apex has the highest V/Q ratio in the upright lung — well-ventilated but poorly perfused. The resulting high alveolar PO₂ provides ideal conditions for this aerobic organism to proliferate.

whyWrongByChoice.A: The apex has relatively good ventilation but poor perfusion. The high V/Q ratio means the apex has high (not low) oxygen tension.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Alveolar membrane thickness does not vary significantly between apex and base. The apex does not have thicker membranes.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Blood flow is lowest at the apex, not highest. The apex receives less perfusion due to gravity.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Alveolar macrophages are present throughout the lung, including the apex. Their presence does not prevent TB reactivation in this oxygen-rich environment.

questions.stem: A patient with left-sided heart failure develops pulmonary edema. The primary mechanism causing fluid accumulation in the lungs in this condition is:

choices.label: D
choices.text: Impaired lymphatic drainage from mediastinal tumors

explanation.summary: In left-sided heart failure, blood backs up into the pulmonary veins and capillaries, raising pulmonary capillary hydrostatic pressure above 25 mmHg. When hydrostatic pressure exceeds the opposing colloid osmotic pressure (~25-28 mmHg), net fluid movement occurs out of the capillaries into the lung interstitium and alveoli.
explanation.whyCorrect: Left heart failure impairs left ventricular emptying, causing blood to pool in the pulmonary venous system. This elevates pulmonary capillary hydrostatic pressure beyond the threshold that oncotic pressure can oppose, driving fluid into the lung tissue — hydrostatic (cardiogenic) pulmonary edema.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Increased capillary permeability causes non-cardiogenic (permeability) edema, as seen in ARDS or sepsis. Left heart failure causes hydrostatic edema from elevated pressures.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Surfactant deficiency causes atelectasis and increased surface tension but is not the primary mechanism of pulmonary edema in heart failure.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Impaired lymphatic drainage can contribute to edema but is caused by tumors or lymphangitis, not left heart failure.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Decreased oncotic pressure (from hypoalbuminemia, liver disease) can cause edema but is a separate mechanism from the hydrostatic edema of left heart failure.

questions.stem: In West's Zone 3 at the base of the upright lung, blood flow is determined by the pressure gradient between which two pressures?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Alveolar pressure and venous pressure (PA − Pv)

choices.label: B
choices.text: Arterial pressure and alveolar pressure (Pa − PA)

choices.label: C
choices.text: Arterial pressure and venous pressure (Pa − Pv)

explanation.summary: In Zone 3 (base), both arterial and venous pressures exceed alveolar pressure (Pa > Pv > PA), so capillaries are fully open. Blood flow follows standard hemodynamic principles, determined by the arterial-venous pressure gradient (Pa − Pv).
explanation.whyCorrect: Since alveolar pressure does not compress the capillaries in Zone 3, the flow is governed by the classic driving pressure: arterial pressure minus venous pressure (Pa − Pv). This is the normal hemodynamic principle that applies when vessels are fully open.

whyWrongByChoice.A: PA − Pv would be relevant if alveolar pressure affected flow, but in Zone 3, alveolar pressure is the lowest of the three and does not compress vessels.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Pa − PA determines flow in Zone 2, where alveolar pressure partially compresses the downstream capillary. In Zone 3, venous pressure exceeds alveolar pressure.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Intrapleural and atmospheric pressures determine lung inflation (transpulmonary pressure), not blood flow distribution within West's zones.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Transpulmonary pressure keeps alveoli inflated but does not directly determine blood flow in Zone 3.

questions.stem: The pulmonary circulation serves as a filter for the systemic circulation. Which of the following does the pulmonary capillary bed filter?

explanation.summary: Since the lungs receive 100% of cardiac output, the pulmonary capillary bed functions as a filter that traps small blood clots (thrombi) from the venous system before they can reach the systemic arterial circulation. Local thrombolytic processes then break down these clots.
explanation.whyCorrect: The extensive pulmonary capillary network captures small venous thrombi, preventing them from embolizing to the brain, heart, or other organs. The pulmonary endothelium also has thrombolytic activity that helps dissolve these trapped clots.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Red blood cells pass freely through the pulmonary capillaries. They are not filtered out; they are needed for gas exchange.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Carbon dioxide is removed from the blood by gas exchange (diffusion into alveoli), not by filtration through the capillary bed.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Plasma proteins are not filtered by the pulmonary capillaries. They remain in the blood and contribute to oncotic pressure.
whyWrongByChoice.E: White blood cells pass through the pulmonary capillaries and are not filtered out. Some immune cells (like alveolar macrophages) reside in the lungs, but circulating WBCs are not trapped.

questions.stem: Hypoxic pulmonary vasoconstriction (HPV) is primarily triggered by which of the following?

explanation.summary: HPV is primarily triggered by low alveolar PO₂ (PAO₂), not arterial hypoxemia. The local alveolar oxygen tension is sensed by the smooth muscle cells of pulmonary arterioles surrounding those alveoli, triggering vasoconstriction to divert blood to better-ventilated regions.
explanation.whyCorrect: The primary stimulus for HPV is alveolar hypoxia (low PAO₂). The pulmonary arteriolar smooth muscle cells respond to the local oxygen environment in the alveoli adjacent to them, rather than to the oxygen content of the blood flowing through them.

whyWrongByChoice.A: While arterial hypoxemia (low PaO₂) can contribute to HPV, the primary trigger is alveolar hypoxia (low PAO₂). The distinction is important because HPV is a local response to regional alveolar conditions.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Elevated PaCO₂ causes respiratory acidosis and can augment HPV, but it is not the primary trigger. The primary stimulus is low PAO₂.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Low pH (acidosis) can potentiate HPV but is not the primary trigger. HPV is fundamentally an oxygen-sensing response.
whyWrongByChoice.E: High PACO₂ may contribute to local hypoxia via the alveolar gas equation, but the direct trigger for HPV is the low PAO₂, not the elevated CO₂.

questions.stem: Non-cardiogenic pulmonary edema (permeability edema), as seen in ARDS, differs from cardiogenic edema in that the edema fluid is:

choices.label: C
choices.text: Identical in composition to cardiogenic edema

choices.label: D
choices.text: Composed primarily of red blood cells

explanation.summary: In non-cardiogenic (permeability) edema, damaged capillary walls allow protein-rich fluid to leak into the interstitium and alveoli. This protein-rich exudate distinguishes it from cardiogenic edema, which produces a protein-poor transudate due to elevated hydrostatic pressure alone.
explanation.whyCorrect: Permeability edema occurs when the capillary endothelium is damaged (by infection, inflammation, or toxic injury), allowing proteins and fluid to escape. The leaked proteins also worsen edema by increasing interstitial oncotic pressure and disrupting surfactant function.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Low-protein transudate is characteristic of cardiogenic (hydrostatic) edema, where the capillary walls are intact and only fluid is pushed out by high pressure.
whyWrongByChoice.C: The two types of edema differ in protein content. Cardiogenic edema produces transudate; permeability edema produces protein-rich exudate.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Pulmonary edema fluid is not primarily composed of red blood cells. While alveolar hemorrhage can occur in severe cases, the edema is primarily fluid and protein.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Non-cardiogenic edema, especially in ARDS, typically contains inflammatory cells (neutrophils, macrophages) as part of the inflammatory response that caused the capillary damage.

questions.stem: According to Poiseuille's law, resistance is inversely proportional to the fourth power of the vessel radius. This means that doubling the radius of pulmonary vessels would reduce resistance by a factor of:

explanation.summary: Per Poiseuille's law, R ∝ 1/r⁴. If the radius doubles (2r), resistance decreases by 2⁴ = 16-fold. This explains why even small increases in pulmonary vessel diameter through recruitment and distension can dramatically reduce pulmonary vascular resistance.
explanation.whyCorrect: Poiseuille's law shows that resistance is inversely proportional to the fourth power of the radius (R ∝ 1/r⁴). Doubling the radius: (1/2⁴) = 1/16, meaning resistance decreases by a factor of 16.

whyWrongByChoice.A: A factor of 2 would apply if resistance were inversely proportional to the first power of the radius, not the fourth.
whyWrongByChoice.B: A factor of 4 (2²) would apply if resistance were inversely proportional to the second power of the radius.
whyWrongByChoice.C: A factor of 8 (2³) would apply if resistance were inversely proportional to the third power of the radius.
whyWrongByChoice.E: A factor of 32 (2⁵) would apply if resistance were inversely proportional to the fifth power of the radius. Poiseuille's law specifies the fourth power.

questions.stem: A mountaineer ascending to 5,000 meters develops high-altitude pulmonary edema (HAPE). Which of the following is the primary mechanism?

choices.label: B
choices.text: Uneven HPV causing focal capillary overperfusion and damage

choices.label: C
choices.text: Decreased atmospheric pressure reducing plasma oncotic pressure

choices.label: D
choices.text: Surfactant washout from cold air exposure

choices.label: E
choices.text: Increased lymphatic drainage overwhelming venous return

explanation.summary: At high altitude, global hypoxia triggers HPV. However, the vasoconstriction is often uneven — some regions constrict more than others, causing excessive blood flow through less-constricted areas. This focal overperfusion at elevated pressures damages capillaries and causes protein-rich fluid leakage (HAPE).
explanation.whyCorrect: HAPE results from uneven HPV across the lungs. Areas with less vasoconstriction receive disproportionately high blood flow at elevated pressures, damaging the fragile pulmonary capillaries and causing them to leak fluid and protein into the alveoli.

whyWrongByChoice.A: HAPE is not caused by left ventricular failure. It is a non-cardiogenic edema caused by pulmonary vascular changes from hypoxic vasoconstriction.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Atmospheric pressure changes do not affect plasma oncotic pressure. Oncotic pressure is determined by plasma protein concentration, not atmospheric pressure.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Surfactant is not washed out by cold air. While surfactant dysfunction can worsen HAPE, the primary mechanism is uneven HPV with capillary damage.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Lymphatic drainage does not overwhelm venous return at altitude. HAPE is due to increased capillary pressure and permeability from uneven HPV.

questions.stem: In Starling's equation for fluid balance across pulmonary capillaries, which force opposes fluid movement out of the capillaries?

explanation.summary: Plasma colloid osmotic (oncotic) pressure, generated primarily by albumin, acts to pull fluid back into the capillaries, opposing the outward push of hydrostatic pressure. Under normal conditions, these forces are balanced, preventing pulmonary edema.
explanation.whyCorrect: Oncotic pressure from plasma proteins (mainly albumin) creates an osmotic gradient that draws fluid from the interstitium back into the capillaries. This force opposes the hydrostatic pressure that pushes fluid out, maintaining fluid balance across the capillary wall.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Capillary hydrostatic pressure pushes fluid out of the capillaries, not back in. It is the force that promotes filtration, not the opposing force.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Alveolar surface tension pulls the alveolar wall inward. While it can affect fluid dynamics, it is not the primary force opposing fluid movement out of capillaries in Starling's equation.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Interstitial hydrostatic pressure is generally near zero or slightly negative in the lungs. It does not provide a major opposing force to capillary filtration.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Alveolar air pressure affects alveolar dynamics but is not the force that pulls fluid back into capillaries in the Starling equation.

questions.stem: The pulmonary circulation can accommodate up to a five-fold increase in cardiac output during exercise with only a minimal rise in pulmonary arterial pressure. This is primarily due to which property of the pulmonary vasculature?

choices.label: C
choices.text: High compliance with capacity for recruitment and distension

choices.label: E
choices.text: Low total blood volume in the pulmonary circuit

explanation.summary: The thin-walled, highly compliant pulmonary vessels can accommodate large increases in blood flow through recruitment (opening of previously closed capillaries) and distension (expansion of open vessels). This keeps pressures below 25 mmHg even during intense exercise.
explanation.whyCorrect: High compliance allows pulmonary vessels to expand with minimal pressure increase. Recruitment and distension increase the total cross-sectional area of the vascular bed, dramatically reducing resistance and preventing pressure overload of the right ventricle and the delicate alveolar capillaries.

whyWrongByChoice.A: High vascular resistance would impede flow accommodation. The pulmonary circulation has low resistance, which is further reduced during exercise.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Thick muscular walls characterize systemic arteries, not pulmonary vessels. The thin walls of pulmonary arteries contribute to their high compliance.
whyWrongByChoice.D: The pulmonary circulation is less dependent on autonomic vasoregulation compared to the systemic circulation. The accommodation of increased flow is primarily a passive mechanical response.
whyWrongByChoice.E: The ability to accommodate increased flow is due to compliance and vascular reserve (recruitment/distension), not low blood volume.

questions.stem: In the supine position, how does the distribution of pulmonary blood flow change compared to the upright position?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Blood flow becomes completely uniform throughout the lung

choices.label: B
choices.text: The apex-to-base gradient is eliminated but posterior regions receive slightly more flow

choices.label: C
choices.text: Blood flow remains identical to the upright position

choices.label: D
choices.text: Blood flow shifts entirely to the anterior regions

choices.label: E
choices.text: Apex blood flow increases dramatically while base flow decreases

explanation.summary: In the supine position, the vertical height of the lungs is reduced, diminishing the gravitational effect on the apex-to-base gradient. Blood flow becomes more uniform, with slightly greater perfusion to the posterior (dependent) regions due to the remaining gravitational effect.
explanation.whyCorrect: Lying supine reduces the vertical distance over which gravity acts on the pulmonary vasculature. The traditional apex-to-base gradient largely disappears, and the new dependent regions (posterior parts of the lung) receive slightly more blood flow than the non-dependent (anterior) regions.

whyWrongByChoice.A: While flow becomes more uniform, it is not perfectly uniform. The posterior dependent regions still receive slightly more blood flow due to residual gravitational effects.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Blood flow distribution changes significantly with body position. The upright apex-to-base gradient shifts to a posterior-to-anterior gradient in the supine position.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Blood flow shifts to the dependent (posterior) regions in the supine position, not to the anterior regions.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Apex flow does increase relative to upright positioning, but not dramatically. The primary change is the overall reduction in the apex-to-base gradient.

questions.stem: The normal mean pulmonary arterial pressure is approximately:

explanation.summary: The normal mean pulmonary arterial pressure is approximately 15 mmHg, reflecting the low-pressure nature of the pulmonary circulation. Pulmonary hypertension is defined as a mean pulmonary arterial pressure ≥ 25 mmHg at rest.
explanation.whyCorrect: The mean pulmonary arterial pressure of ~15 mmHg is approximately one-sixth of the systemic mean arterial pressure (~95 mmHg). This low pressure protects the thin-walled, delicate alveolar capillaries from damage while still being sufficient to perfuse the lungs.

whyWrongByChoice.A: 5 mmHg is approximately the normal right atrial pressure, not the mean pulmonary arterial pressure.
whyWrongByChoice.C: 50 mmHg would indicate severe pulmonary hypertension, well above the normal ~15 mmHg.
whyWrongByChoice.D: 95 mmHg is the normal systemic mean arterial pressure, not pulmonary. The pulmonary circuit operates at much lower pressures.
whyWrongByChoice.E: 120 mmHg is a systemic systolic pressure. Pulmonary pressures are far lower.

questions.stem: Pulmonary edema progresses through stages as protective mechanisms are overwhelmed. What is the first stage of pulmonary edema before alveolar flooding?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Alveolar edema with impaired gas exchange

choices.label: B
